questions.question: Who among the following decides the allocation of portfolios among Indian state ministers?
questions.option_2: Chief Justice of the High Court
questions.solution:
Allocation of Portfolios

While the Governor technically appoints ministers, they do so only on the advice of the Chief Minister. The power to allocate or reshuffle portfolios (departments) among the ministers rests entirely with the Chief Minister, who is the head of the state council of ministers.

questions.question: What is a key obligation of citizens toward fellow citizens and society?
questions.option_1: Serve in the military automatically and responsibly
questions.option_3: Participate morally and socially, not just legally
questions.solution:
Obligations of Citizens

In a democratic society, a citizen's duty goes beyond mere legal compliance. A key obligation is to participate morally and socially—this includes respecting the rights of others, fostering communal harmony, and engaging in the democratic process responsibly, which forms the essence of Fundamental Duties.

questions.question: In which year was the National Disaster Response Force (NDRF) formed under the Disaster Management Act of 2005?
questions.solution:
National Disaster Response Force (NDRF)

Although the Disaster Management Act was passed in 2005, the NDRF was officially constituted in 2006. It is a specialized force capable of responding to natural and man-made disasters, operating under the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA).

questions.question: Which example best reflects the need for the Constitution in daily life?
questions.option_1: Buying groceries from a local store
questions.option_2: Class deciding rules for representation and resource sharing
questions.solution:
Constitution in Daily Life

The need for a Constitution is best seen when a group or class decides on rules for representation and resource sharing. It sets the 'rules of the game,' ensuring that power is not arbitrary and that resources are distributed according to agreed-upon principles of justice.

questions.question: Which Constitutional Amendment added the words 'Socialist', 'Secular' and 'Integrity' to the Preamble of the Indian Constitution?
questions.solution:
The 42nd Amendment (Mini-Constitution)

The 42nd Amendment Act of 1976 added three new words to the Preamble: 'Socialist', 'Secular', and 'Integrity'. It remains the only time the Preamble has been amended in the history of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following cases CANNOT be taken up by a Lok Adalat?
questions.solution:
Lok Adalats and Criminal Offences

Lok Adalats can settle civil cases and compoundable criminal offences (where the law allows for a compromise). However, they cannot take up non-compoundable criminal offences, as these are considered serious crimes against the state that cannot be settled outside the regular court process.

questions.question: In which of the following years was the National Telecom Policy (NTP), that envisaged the objective of one public call office (PCO) for every 500 persons in the urban areas, formulated?
questions.solution:
National Telecom Policy (NTP) 1994

The NTP 1994 was a landmark policy that opened the telecom sector to private participation. One of its key social objectives was to ensure the availability of telephone services on demand and to achieve the target of one PCO for every 500 persons in urban areas.

questions.question: Which of the following Articles deals with the Right against Exploitation?
questions.solution:
Right against Exploitation

Articles 23 and 24 of the Indian Constitution protect citizens from exploitation. Article 23 prohibits human trafficking and forced labor (begar), while Article 24 prohibits the employment of children below 14 years in hazardous industries.

questions.question: Which Article was inserted into the Constitution by the 103ʳᵈ Amendment Act to provide for EWS reservation?
questions.solution:
EWS Reservation (Articles 15 & 16)

The 103rd Amendment Act (2019) introduced 10% reservation for the Economically Weaker Sections (EWS). It inserted Article 15(6) to allow for reservations in educational institutions and Article 16(6) for reservations in government appointments.

questions.question: In which year was the Second Pay Commission appointed in India?
questions.solution:
Second Pay Commission

The Second Pay Commission was appointed in 1957 under the chairmanship of Jaganath Das. It submitted its report in 1959, focusing on the pay structures and retirement benefits of Central Government employees.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct about Article 323B of the Constitution?
questions.option_1: It allows the creation of tribunals only for the armed forces.
questions.option_2: It permits tribunals for matters like taxation, land reforms, and labour disputes.
questions.option_3: It restricts tribunals to environmental cases only.
questions.option_4: It empowers states to establish tribunals for local governance.
questions.solution:
Article 323B: Specific Tribunals

While Article 323A is exclusively for Administrative Tribunals, Article 323B empowers the appropriate Legislature to create tribunals for other specific matters such as taxation, foreign exchange, labor disputes, land reforms, and essential commodities.

questions.question: Which of the following subjects falls under the Concurrent List?
questions.solution:
Concurrent List: Criminal Law

Criminal Law and Procedure fall under the Concurrent List (Seventh Schedule). This allows both the Parliament and State Legislatures to make laws on crimes, though central laws generally prevail in case of conflict. (Banking and Defence are Union subjects; Police is a State subject).

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution mentions the Council of Ministers to aid and advise the President?
questions.solution:
Article 74: Aid and Advice

Article 74 states that there shall be a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister at the head to aid and advise the President. The President is constitutionally required to act in accordance with this advice (as per the 42nd and 44th Amendments).

questions.question: Which of the following Directive Principles of State Policy is provided under Article 40 of the Indian Constitution?
questions.option_2: Provision for a uniform civil code
questions.option_3: Promotion of cottage industries in rural areas
questions.option_4: Organisation of agriculture and animal husbandry
questions.solution:
Article 40: Village Panchayats

Article 40, a Gandhian principle in the DPSP, directs the State to organize Village Panchayats and endow them with such powers and authority as may be necessary to enable them to function as units of self-government.

questions.question: Which schedule of the Citizenship Act, 1955, contains the qualifications for naturalisation?
questions.solution:
Qualifications for Naturalisation

The Third Schedule of the Citizenship Act, 1955, details the specific qualifications for naturalisation. These include requirements regarding residency in India, character, and knowledge of a language specified in the Eighth Schedule.

questions.question: Which of the following options correctly lists the composition of the State Executive in India?
questions.option_1: Governor, Chief Minister and Council of Ministers
questions.option_3: Governor, Chief Minister and State Legislative Assembly members
questions.option_4: Governor, Deputy Minister and State Council of Advisors
questions.solution:
The State Executive

The State Executive consists of the Governor, the Chief Minister, and the Council of Ministers. In states like Bihar or Maharashtra, it also includes the Advocate General of the State. It does not include the President or the Legislative Assembly members.

questions.question: If an RTI applicant is aggrieved by the Public Information Officer’s decision (or lack of response), within how many days must the first appeal be filed under Section 19 of the RTI Act?
questions.solution:
RTI: First Appeal Timeline

If an applicant does not receive a decision within the time specified or is aggrieved by the decision of the PIO, they may file a First Appeal within 30 days to an officer senior in rank to the PIO (the First Appellate Authority).

questions.question: Select the correct statement regarding the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) of India.
questions.option_1: It is a constitutional body formed by a Constitutional Amendment, with powers equal to the Supreme Court.
questions.option_2: The NHRC has direct powers to prosecute and punish public officials for human rights abuses.
questions.option_3: It was established in 1993 by an Act of Parliament to investigate and recommend on human rights violations.
questions.option_4: The Chairperson of the NHRC is the Prime Minister of India.
questions.solution:
Nature of the NHRC

The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) is a statutory body, not a constitutional one. It was established in 1993 under the Protection of Human Rights Act. Its role is recommendatory; it can investigate abuses but cannot directly punish officials.

questions.question: According to the principle of collective responsibility in India’s parliamentary system, a successful no-confidence motion against a single Union Minister requires which action?
questions.option_1: The entire Council of Ministers must resign.
questions.option_2: Only the targeted minister must resign.
questions.option_3: The President dismisses only the targeted minister.
questions.option_4: The Lok Sabha is immediately dissolved.
questions.solution:
Collective Responsibility

The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha (or Vidhan Sabha). This means if a no-confidence motion is passed against even one minister (or the PM), it is viewed as a loss of confidence in the government, and the entire Council of Ministers must resign.

questions.question: Which authority can dissolve the Council of Ministers at the state level?
questions.solution:
Dissolution of State Council

The Governor has the power to dissolve the Council of Ministers at the state level if it loses the majority in the Legislative Assembly and fails to prove its strength on the floor of the House.

questions.question: Which of the following Ministries is responsible for granting Indian citizenship by naturalisation?
questions.solution:
MHA and Citizenship

The Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) is the nodal ministry responsible for matters related to Indian Citizenship. It processes applications for citizenship via registration or naturalisation and maintains the records of Indian nationals.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a role of the President of India?
questions.solution:
Electoral Role of the President

The President appoints the Chief Election Commissioner and other commissioners, but they do not conduct elections. That role belongs exclusively to the Election Commission of India (ECI), which is an independent constitutional body.

questions.question: Which of the following Articles deals with the rights of citizenship at the commencement of the Indian Constitution?
questions.solution:
Article 5: Starting Point

Article 5 defines who was a citizen at the commencement of the Constitution. It covers those domiciled in India who were born in India, or whose parents were born here, or who were resident for five years preceding January 1950.

questions.question: Which of the following best reflects Judicial Activism?
questions.option_1: Voluntary interference by the judiciary in public interest
questions.solution:
Judicial Activism

Judicial Activism refers to the proactive role played by the judiciary in protecting the rights of citizens and promoting justice. It often manifests through Public Interest Litigation (PIL), where courts take up issues on their own (suo motu) or based on a third-party petition.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct regarding the Tenth Schedule of the Indian Constitution?
questions.option_1: It provides for disqualification of members on grounds of defection.
questions.option_2: It defines the distribution of powers between the Union and States.
questions.option_3: It contains provisions regarding Official Languages.
questions.option_4: It deals with the allocation of seats in the Rajya Sabha.
questions.solution:
Anti-Defection Law

The Tenth Schedule (Anti-Defection Law) is designed to prevent political instability caused by legislators changing parties for personal gain. It empowers the Speaker/Chairman to disqualify a member on the grounds of defection.
